content.text: What Industrial and OT Agents Are
Industrial AI agents are software agents that operate at the boundary between data-driven AI reasoning and physical industrial processes.
Operational Technology (OT)

is the collective term for hardware and software that monitors and controls physical devices: programmable logic controllers (PLCs), distributed control systems (DCS), supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) systems, and the sensors and actuators that connect them to the real world.

Unlike IT agents that operate on stateless, fault-tolerant services, OT agents must reason about systems that are always running, often cannot be restarted, and where a wrong control action can cause physical harm. The core design question for any industrial AI agent is therefore: what can it observe, recommend, or control — and what hard boundaries must it never cross?
The IT/OT Convergence

Industrial facilities have historically kept IT networks (office, cloud, analytics) and OT networks (plant floor, control systems) air-gapped or separated by strict firewalls. Over the last decade, economic pressure to use real-time production data for optimisation and maintenance has driven a convergence: OT data now flows to cloud platforms, and AI agents in the cloud produce recommendations that operators feed back into the plant.

attrs.alt: IT/OT convergence: AI agents sit in the IT layer and read OT data via historian and OPC-UA bridge
attrs.title: IT / OT Convergence in Industrial AI

content.text: The bridge between layers is typically an
OT data historian
(OSIsoft PI, AVEVA Historian, or similar) that buffers time-series data from the control network and exposes it to the IT side via REST or
OPC-UA
interfaces. AI agents consume this buffered data rather than connecting directly to PLCs or DCS controllers.
Industrial Domains Where Agents Add Value
The same agent architecture patterns recur across multiple heavy-industry verticals. The differentiation is in the domain knowledge baked into the agent's tools, prompts, and constraints.
Domain
Representative agent tasks
Key OT data sources
Manufacturing
Predictive maintenance, quality control, OEE optimisation
Vibration sensors, machine counters, SCADA tags
Oil & Gas
Pipeline leak detection, compressor health, flare optimisation
Flow meters, pressure transmitters, gas analysers
Power & Utilities
Grid load balancing, transformer health, fault detection
SCADA/EMS, smart meters, PMUs
Water & Wastewater
Pump scheduling, chemical dosing, SCADA alarming
Flow, pressure, turbidity, pH sensors
Mining
Equipment availability, ore feed optimisation, blast planning
Shovel sensors, belt scales, drill telemetry
Process Chemicals
Batch quality prediction, energy efficiency, recipe optimisation
Reactor temperatures, flow rates, lab LIMS
OT Environment Constraints
Designing agents for OT environments requires understanding constraints that have no equivalent in cloud software:
Availability over agility.

OT systems run continuously. A production plant cannot be taken offline for a software update the way a web service can. Agents must tolerate systems that are 10-30 years old, running unsupported operating systems, with firmware that cannot be changed.
Deterministic timing.

PLCs execute scan cycles in fixed milliseconds (1-100 ms is typical). Any agent that interacts with or monitors control systems must respect these timing constraints — a delayed read or a time-out does not propagate safely the way it does in a microservice.
Safety above all else.

Industrial facilities operate under IEC 61511 (process safety) and IEC 62443 (cybersecurity) standards. Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) have certified response times and must never be overridden by an AI agent. The agent's authority ends at the SIS boundary.
Limited compute on the edge.

Many OT environments have no cloud connectivity. Agents that must run at the edge are constrained to smaller models or inference optimisations (quantisation, ONNX) that are incompatible with naive cloud-LLM approaches.
High change cost.
Changing a setpoint in a chemical plant requires a Management of Change (MoC) procedure. Agents that propose configuration changes must produce auditable reasoning, not just a recommendation.
The Purdue Model and Network Segmentation
The
Purdue Enterprise Reference Architecture
provides a layered model for industrial network segmentation. Understanding which Purdue level the agent operates at determines what data it can access and what actions it is permitted to take.

attrs.alt: Purdue model levels 0-5 with AI agent access rights per level
attrs.title: Purdue Model: Where AI Agents Operate Safely

content.text: The key practical rule:
AI agents live at Levels 4 and 5

. They read time-series data from the historian at Level 3 via a one-way data diode or firewall-controlled interface. They never have a network path to PLCs (Level 1) or physical instrumentation (Level 0). Advisory outputs are displayed on Level 3 operator consoles, but only human operators write setpoints to Level 2 and below.
Real-Time Requirements and Determinism

Agents reading from an OT historian are operating on buffered data — there is an inherent delay between a process event and the agent seeing it. Typical historian latency is 1-5 seconds. This is acceptable for predictive maintenance (where a bearing failure develops over hours) but unacceptable for process control (where a pressure excursion must be contained in milliseconds).
The practical rule: AI agents are appropriate for
decisions on a timescale of minutes to days
. Real-time closed-loop control (sub-second) stays with PLCs and DCS systems. An agent recommends an adjusted operating envelope; the DCS enforces it at scan-cycle speed.
Decision timescale
Appropriate system
Example
PLC / safety relay
Emergency shutdown on over-pressure
DCS control loop
Temperature setpoint adjustment
1 s – 1 min
Advanced process control (APC)
Model-predictive control
1 min – 1 h
AI agent (advisory)
Recommended setpoint change, quality prediction
1 h – days
AI agent (planning)
Maintenance scheduling, supply chain optimisation
Safety Instrumented Systems and Agent Boundaries
A
Safety Instrumented System (SIS)

is a certified, independent system that brings a process to a safe state when defined hazard conditions are met. SIS systems are governed by IEC 61511 and carry a Safety Integrity Level (SIL) rating. SIL 2 or SIL 3 systems are architecturally independent from the basic process control system (BPCS) and from any AI agent layer.
Agent design rule: an AI agent must never:
Write to any process tag that is in the SIS's input/output list.
Recommend bypassing or inhibiting a SIS function, even temporarily.
Communicate over a network path that routes through the SIS network segment.
Claim uncertainty about SIS-related conditions without escalating immediately to a human operator.
When an agent detects a condition that suggests an impending SIS trip — for example, rising hydrogen sulfide concentration approaching the SIS setpoint — the correct action is to
alert the operator immediately
, explain the reasoning, and stop. The agent does not attempt to prevent the SIS from acting.
Reading OT Data via OPC-UA
OPC Unified Architecture (OPC-UA) is the standard protocol for read/write access to OT data. The
opcua-asyncio
library provides async OPC-UA client support in Python. AI agents use OPC-UA to read tag values, subscribe to data changes, and (with appropriate permissions at Level 3) write advisory setpoints.

import asyncio
from asyncua import Client, ua

OPC_SERVER_URL = "opc.tcp://historian-bridge.plant.local:4840"

# Tag nodeIds — use the server's address space browser to find these
TAGS = {
    "motor_bearing_temp_c":  "ns=2;s=Plant.MotorLine1.BearingTemperature",
    "vibration_rms_mm_s":    "ns=2;s=Plant.MotorLine1.VibrationRMS",
    "motor_current_a":       "ns=2;s=Plant.MotorLine1.Current",
    "motor_speed_rpm":       "ns=2;s=Plant.MotorLine1.Speed",
}

async def read_tag_values() -> dict[str, float]:
    """Read current values for all monitored tags from OPC-UA server."""
    async with Client(url=OPC_SERVER_URL) as client:
        results = {}
        for name, node_id in TAGS.items():
            node = client.get_node(node_id)
            dv = await node.read_data_value()
            if dv.StatusCode.is_good():
                results[name] = float(dv.Value.Value)
            else:
                results[name] = None  # tag in bad quality — handle upstream
        return results


async def subscribe_tag_changes(callback, poll_interval_ms: int = 500):
    """Subscribe to data changes; callback(name, value, timestamp) is called on each change."""
    class DataChangeHandler:
        def __init__(self, name_map):
            self.name_map = name_map  # node_id -> friendly name

        async def datachange_notification(self, node, val, data):
            node_id_str = str(node.nodeid)
            name = self.name_map.get(node_id_str, node_id_str)
            ts = data.monitored_item.Value.SourceTimestamp
            await callback(name, float(val), ts)

    reversed_tags = {v: k for k, v in TAGS.items()}
    handler = DataChangeHandler(reversed_tags)

    async with Client(url=OPC_SERVER_URL) as client:
        sub = await client.create_subscription(poll_interval_ms, handler)
        nodes = [client.get_node(nid) for nid in TAGS.values()]
        await sub.subscribe_data_change(nodes)
        # Keep running until cancelled
        try:
            while True:
                await asyncio.sleep(1)
        finally:
            await sub.unsubscribe(nodes)
            await sub.delete()


# Example: one-shot read
async def main():
    values = await read_tag_values()
    for name, val in values.items():
        print(f"{name}: {val}")

asyncio.run(main())
Building a Predictive Maintenance Agent

Predictive maintenance (PdM) is the most common entry point for AI agents in industrial environments. The agent reads sensor data, reasons about asset health, and generates maintenance work orders before a failure occurs — replacing calendar-based schedules with condition-based ones.
The architecture has three components: a
data collection layer
that polls OPC-UA or historian REST APIs; a
health scoring model
(a statistical or ML model trained on historical failure data); and an
agent loop
that uses an LLM to interpret the health score, enrich it with maintenance history context, and produce a structured work order recommendation.

import json
import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# --- Health scoring (placeholder; replace with trained model) ---------------

@dataclass
class AssetReading:
    asset_id: str
    bearing_temp_c: float
    vibration_rms: float       # mm/s
    current_a: float
    speed_rpm: float
    timestamp: datetime

@dataclass
class HealthScore:
    asset_id: str
    score: float               # 0.0 (failed) – 1.0 (healthy)
    risk_level: str            # 'low' | 'medium' | 'high' | 'critical'
    contributing_factors: list[str]

def compute_health_score(reading: AssetReading) -> HealthScore:
    """Rule-based health score — replace with ML model output in production."""
    factors = []
    penalty = 0.0

    if reading.bearing_temp_c > 85:
        factors.append(f"High bearing temperature: {reading.bearing_temp_c:.1f}°C (limit 85°C)")
        penalty += 0.3 if reading.bearing_temp_c > 95 else 0.15

    if reading.vibration_rms > 7.1:   # ISO 10816-3 Zone D
        factors.append(f"Vibration in danger zone: {reading.vibration_rms:.2f} mm/s (limit 7.1 mm/s)")
        penalty += 0.35 if reading.vibration_rms > 11.0 else 0.2

    if reading.current_a > 1.15 * 48.0:   # >115% of rated current
        factors.append(f"Motor overcurrent: {reading.current_a:.1f} A (rated 48 A)")
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Anomaly Detection on Industrial Telemetry
Predictive maintenance targets known failure modes. Anomaly detection targets
unknown unknowns

— patterns that deviate from established baselines without necessarily matching any labelled failure signature. In industrial environments, anomalies can indicate equipment degradation, process upsets, or cybersecurity events.

A practical approach pairs a lightweight statistical model (e.g. a rolling z-score or isolation forest) for continuous screening with an LLM agent for contextual interpretation. The statistical model runs at poll frequency; the LLM is only invoked when a score crosses a threshold, reducing token cost dramatically.
Rolling baseline.

Maintain a rolling window of recent values (e.g. 24 h for slow processes, 1 h for fast ones). Flag a tag when its current value exceeds 3 standard deviations from the rolling mean while the process is in a steady operating regime (not during startups or transitions, which generate their own variance).
Multi-variate context.

Single-tag anomalies have high false-positive rates. An agent that cross-checks a flagged tag against correlated tags — if bearing temperature rises but current and vibration do not, the thermocouple is more likely faulty than the bearing — produces more useful alerts.
Suppression during transitions.
Provide the agent with a process state signal (e.g. a SCADA tag indicating startup/shutdown/normal) so it can suppress anomaly alerts when variance is expected.
Process Optimisation Agents

Process optimisation agents recommend changes to operating setpoints to improve energy efficiency, throughput, or product quality. They are distinct from the real-time controllers (APC, MPC) that execute those changes at millisecond timescales. The agent operates at the planning layer: given current operating conditions and recent process history, it identifies opportunities and presents them to operators as validated recommendations with supporting evidence.
A typical workflow:
Agent polls the historian for the last 4 hours of operating data.
Agent compares current key performance indicators (KPIs) against the best-performing historical periods with similar feed conditions.
If a gap is identified, the agent reasons about which setpoint changes have correlated with better performance — using tools to query historical experiments or CMMS notes.
The agent presents a recommendation including: proposed setpoint, expected KPI impact, confidence, and the historical basis for the recommendation.
A human operator reviews and optionally enters the setpoint change into the DCS. The agent does not write to the DCS directly.
Digital Twins as Agent Environments
A
digital twin

is a simulation model of a physical asset or process that runs in software and is continuously updated with live sensor data. For AI agents, digital twins serve two purposes: they provide a safe sandbox for testing recommended actions before applying them to the physical plant, and they extend the agent's observation space with derived quantities that are too dangerous or expensive to measure directly (e.g. internal furnace temperatures, subsurface reservoir pressure).

Agent interactions with digital twins follow the same tool-call pattern as any other tool. The agent calls a simulation API with proposed setpoints; the twin returns predicted outputs; the agent evaluates whether the predicted outcomes are acceptable before committing to a recommendation.
Physics-based twins
(e.g. Aspen Plus, OpenModelica) provide high-fidelity predictions but are slow to run and require expert configuration. Use them for high-stakes setpoint changes.
Data-driven twins
(ML surrogates trained on historical data) run in milliseconds and are suitable for the agent's inner loop, with physics-based validation as a final check.
Limitation:
a twin is only as good as the data that trained or calibrated it. An agent must not treat twin predictions as ground truth when the process has drifted outside the training distribution.
Human-in-the-Loop for Safety-Critical Recommendations
Industrial AI agents must always present recommendations to human operators for critical actions rather than executing them autonomously. The design pattern is
advisory with operator confirmation
: the agent reasons, produces a structured recommendation, and creates a work order or notification — but the physical change only happens when an authorised human approves and executes it.

This is not just a safety practice; it is a regulatory requirement in most jurisdictions for SIS-adjacent decisions and for changes that fall under a Management of Change (MoC) procedure. Design the agent's output schema to support this workflow:
Recommendation card.

Structured output with fields: proposed change, expected outcome, confidence, supporting evidence, expiry time (how long the recommendation is valid before conditions change), and required approver role.
Escalation trigger.

If the agent identifies a critical condition (e.g. bearing temperature approaching failure threshold) and no human has acknowledged the recommendation within N minutes, it escalates to a higher-tier notification channel.
Audit trail.
Every recommendation, acceptance, rejection, and outcome is logged with timestamps and user IDs. This log is the evidence base for process safety reviews.
Security in Industrial AI Agents

Industrial AI agents introduce a new attack surface at the IT/OT boundary. Attackers who compromise an agent could extract plant operating data, inject false sensor readings into the agent's context, or — in worst-case designs where the agent has write access — manipulate process setpoints.
Threat
Mitigating control
Prompt injection via OT historian data
Sanitise and schema-validate all historian data before inserting into prompts; never insert raw string data from OT sources directly into LLM context
Compromised agent issues malicious setpoint recommendations
All recommendations require human confirmation; agent has no direct write path to DCS or PLCs
Exfiltration of process data via LLM API
Data minimisation — send only the tags the agent needs; strip asset names/locations if the model is hosted externally
MITM on OPC-UA or historian REST link
Mutual TLS on all OT-to-IT data feeds; OPC-UA security mode SignAndEncrypt
Privilege escalation via overly permissive agent credentials
Principle of least privilege — historian read credentials scoped to specific tag groups; separate credential for CMMS write
A key principle specific to industrial AI is
data-at-rest minimisation

. The agent should request a rolling window of recent data — enough for reasoning — rather than maintaining a large historical dataset that would be valuable to an attacker. Context windows are ephemeral; historian query scope should be audited.
Testing Industrial Agents in Simulation
You cannot test an industrial AI agent by running it against a live plant. Build a
process simulator

that replays historical historian data or generates synthetic time-series matching the statistical properties of the real process. The agent runs against this simulated data source with its OPC-UA and historian tool calls mocked.
Compliance and Audit Requirements

Industrial AI agents that influence safety-critical or regulated processes must meet documentation and audit requirements. The relevant standards depend on the jurisdiction and industry but share common expectations around traceability, change management, and validation.
Standard / Framework
Scope
Key requirement for AI agents
IEC 61511 (Functional Safety)
Process industries, SIS design
AI output must not override SIS; agent changes to BPCS setpoints require functional safety assessment
Industrial cybersecurity
Agent credentials, network paths, and data flows must be documented in the security management system
ISO 13485 / FDA 21 CFR Part 11
Pharma / med-device manufacturing
AI recommendations affecting batch records must be electronically signed and auditable
NERC CIP
Bulk electric power systems
AI agents touching energy management systems require change management and access control documentation
ISO 55000 (Asset Management)
All heavy industry
AI-generated maintenance recommendations must be traceable to condition data; work orders must close the loop with observed outcomes

In practice, audit readiness means every agent action is logged with: the triggering observation (tag values, timestamp), the model version and prompt version used, the recommendation produced, the human who approved it, the outcome observed, and a correlation ID linking all entries for a single decision chain. This log must be retained for the duration specified by the relevant standard (commonly 3-5 years for process safety records).
Summary

Industrial and OT agents bridge AI reasoning with physical processes. They operate at Levels 4-5 of the Purdue model and read OT data via historians and OPC-UA bridges — they never have a direct network path to PLCs or safety systems.

OT environments impose constraints absent in cloud software: always-on availability, deterministic timing requirements, SIS independence, and high change-management cost. Agent designs must account for all of these.

The most mature industrial AI agent use cases are predictive maintenance, KPI anomaly detection, and process optimisation recommendations — all operating at timescales of minutes to days, not the sub-second timescales of real-time control.
AI agents must never recommend bypassing or overriding Safety Instrumented Systems. When agent reasoning touches SIS-adjacent conditions, the mandatory action is to alert a human operator and stop.

Security in industrial AI requires prompt-injection hardening (validate and sanitise all OT data before inserting into LLM context), minimal data footprint, mutual TLS on all OT-IT data feeds, and scoped read-only credentials.

Compliance requires every agent recommendation to be logged with full traceability: triggering data, model version, recommendation, approver, and outcome. This audit trail is mandatory under IEC 61511, ISO 55000, and sector-specific regulations.
